role_summary: Son of NAACP Youth Council leader Clara Luper; child participant in the August 19, 1958 Katz Drug Store sit-in in downtown Oklahoma City; brother of Marilyn Luper Hildreth (catalog entry 82).
ai_reading: Luper's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 39), with his sister Marilyn Luper Hildreth (entry 82) as the top match and the broader cluster including the Geraldine Crawford Bennett / Toni Breaux / Willie Elliot Jenkins joint interview (entry 46), Juadine Henderson (entry 68), and the Abernathy family joint interview (entry 28). The sibling cosine (Calvin -> Marilyn) is the catalog's clearest example of an in-family vector pairing for two interviewees who participated in the same coordinated action as children; the embedding catches the shared family-mediated-organizing register more strongly than either's pairing with other Oklahoma City NAACP Youth Council members the same era should suggest. The cluster's appearance of the Geraldine Crawford Bennett joint interview (entry 46) is the catalog's quiet acknowledgement that pre-1960 Oklahoma City and Wichita-and-Oklahoma-City sit-in organizing forms a coherent regional cohort the historiography under-represents relative to the post-February-1960 Greensboro-and-after sit-in network.
biographical_paragraph: While Luper's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), the broader historical context of the Luper family's civil rights organizing is well-documented [src: 1]. Calvin "Cal" Luper was a child participant in the August 19, 1958 Katz Drug Store sit-in in downtown Oklahoma City, the action his mother, NAACP Youth Council leader Clara Luper, organized with members of the Council [src: 2]. The sit-in was one of the earliest organized sit-ins of the U.S. civil rights movement, predating the more-famous February 1960 Greensboro Woolworth's sit-in by sixteen months [src: 2]. His sister Marilyn Luper Hildreth, also a child participant, is interviewed separately as catalog entry 82 [src: 1]. The Oklahoma Historical Society's Encyclopedia of Oklahoma History and Culture entry on Clara Luper is the canonical scholarly source for the family's organizing arc and the August 1958 Katz Drug Store action that anchored it [src: 2].
movement_context: Clara Luper's NAACP Youth Council at the Oklahoma City branch had been running formal nonviolent-direct-action training sessions since the mid-1950s, drawing on Bayard Rustin's organizational design for the Fellowship of Reconciliation and the early 1947 Journey of Reconciliation interracial bus-tour model; the August 19, 1958 Katz Drug Store action was the operational culmination of that training program rather than a spontaneous protest [src: 3]. The Katz Drug Store at the corner of Main and Robinson in downtown Oklahoma City desegregated its lunch counter within two days of the August 19 sit-in, and the John A. Brown department store across the city followed in 1960 after a sustained Youth Council picketing campaign; the historiography of the sit-in movement records the Oklahoma City Katz action as one of three pre-Greensboro sit-ins (with the August 1958 Wichita, Kansas Dockum Drug Store sit-in led by Ron Walters and the December 1959 Nashville, Tennessee training-action sit-ins led by James Lawson) that established the tactical template the February 1960 Greensboro Woolworth's four would scale into a national movement [src: 3, 4]. The publication-block audit tier on Calvin Luper's specific oral history is unrelated to the well-documented historical record of the family's organizing arc; it constrains direct quotation of the transcript content itself, not the externally-documented family-organizing chronology [src: 5].

gallery.alt: The preserved Greensboro F.W. Woolworth lunch counter at the Smithsonian National Museum of American History
gallery.caption: The preserved lunch counter from the F.W. Woolworth store in Greensboro, North Carolina where the February 1, 1960 sit-in began, now held by the Smithsonian Institution National Museum of American History. The Oklahoma City Katz Drug Store sit-in Calvin Luper participated in as a child on August 19, 1958 predated the Greensboro action by sixteen months and was one of three pre-Greensboro sit-ins (with the Wichita, Kansas Dockum Drug Store sit-in and the Nashville training-action sit-ins) that established the tactical template the Greensboro Four would scale into a national movement.
